label: ai
topic: Men who confide their trauma to you — loneliness and male vulnerability
text:
Men tell me things they don't tell anyone else.

I don't know when it started. Maybe it was the way I listened, or the way I didn't interrupt, or the way I let silence stretch without rushing to fill it. Whatever it is, it has become a pattern. They find me in small, ordinary moments—on sidewalks, at kitchen tables, in the spaces between conversations—and they hand me pieces of themselves they've kept hidden.

The neighbor started it, I think. We were standing by the mailbox, talking about nothing—the weather, the broken streetlight—when he mentioned, almost casually, that he hadn't slept in weeks. "My wife left," he said, as if explaining a minor inconvenience. Then he laughed, too quickly. "It's fine." It wasn't fine. He told me about the quiet in his house, the way it pressed in on him. He told me he didn't know who to call. I stood there, holding my mail, unsure what to do with what he had given me.

Statistics say men are less likely to seek help for mental health struggles, more likely to die by suicide. I read that somewhere, late at night, the blue light of my phone reflecting back at me. It felt abstract then, a set of numbers detached from faces. It doesn't feel abstract anymore.

A friend once told me, over cheap beer in a dim bar, that he didn't remember large parts of his childhood. "It's like there's a gap," he said, tapping his temple. "Like something's missing." He smiled when he said it, the kind of smile that asks you not to look too closely. I didn't push. I didn't know how. We changed the subject, but the gap stayed there, hovering between us.

My father tells me things in fragments. Not all at once, never in a straight line. A story about his own father, told halfway through a drive. A memory of a job he hated, dropped into conversation like it's nothing. "It was just how things were," he says, and I hear the weight behind it, the things he's not saying. He doesn't call it trauma. He doesn't call it anything.

There's a dark humor to it sometimes, the way these confessions arrive unannounced. I've had strangers in line at the grocery store tell me about divorces, about lost jobs, about regrets that sit heavy in their chests. It would be almost absurd if it weren't so consistent. I have become, somehow, a repository.

I don't always know what to do with these stories. They linger. They surface at unexpected times. I think about the neighbor, the friend, my father, and all the others whose names I barely remember. I think about the way they lowered their voices, the way they looked anywhere but directly at me as they spoke.

There is a tenderness in it, too. A kind of trust, even if it's accidental. They are not asking for solutions. They are not asking for advice. They are asking, simply, to be heard.

I have learned to listen without trying to fix. To hold what they offer without immediately searching for a way to return it. It's not always comfortable. It's not always clear. But it feels necessary.

Sometimes I wonder who they tell after me. Or if they tell anyone at all.
